title: DLP — Blocked Exfil to Personal Email Domains

compliance.assurance_rationale: CSF 2.0 PR.DS-02 (Data-in-transit protection) — UC monitors data-in-transit encryption and TLS compliance, providing partial evidence that the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data-in-transit are protected.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST CSF PR.DS-02 (Data-in-transit protection) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.7.27: DLP — Blocked Exfil to Personal Email Domains.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.7.27' running on ms:o365:management DLP, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=o365 sourcetype=ms:o365:management Workload=Dlp earliest=-7d
| where match(Operation,"(?i)block") AND match(UserPrincipalName,"@(gmail|yahoo|hotmail)\.com")
| stats count by PolicyName, UserPrincipalName
description: Counts blocked DLP actions toward personal domains proving technical data protection operates.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIST CSF compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes o365 and sourcetypes ms:o365:management. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for DLP — Blocked Exfil to Personal Email Domains. The NIST Cybersecurity Framework tells us what good practice looks like, and we keep evidence we match it.
implementation: (1) Tune allowed domains; (2) HR escalation path; (3) evidence for data handling procedures. Indexes required: `index=o365`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=ms:o365:management`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `ms:o365:management` DLP.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Tune allowed domains; (2) HR escalation path; (3) evidence for data handling procedures.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=o365 sourcetype=ms:o365:management Workload=Dlp earliest=-7d
| where match(Operation,"(?i)block") AND match(UserPrincipalName,"@(gmail|yahoo|hotmail)\.com")
| stats count by PolicyName, UserPrincipalName
```

Understanding this SPL

**DLP — Blocked Exfil to Personal Email Domains (PR.DS-02)** — Counts blocked DLP actions toward personal domains proving technical data protection operates.

Documented **Data sources**: `ms:o365:management` DLP. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Office 365 (Splunkbase 4055). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: o365; **sourcetype**: ms:o365:management. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=o365, sourcetype=ms:o365:management, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Filters the current rows with `where match(Operation,"(?i)block") AND match(UserPrincipalName,"@(gmail|yahoo|hotmail)\.com")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by PolicyName, UserPrincipalName** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Single value
knownFalsePositives: Protect-control failures during planned exception windows, application compatibility testing, or vendor lag.
